title: Listening - Part 3: Smart Cities
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two urban planners, Professor Marcus Thorne and Dr. Sarah Jenkins, about the development of smart cities. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview discussing the integration of technology in urban environments and its social impact.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Welcome. Today we’re discussing 'Smart Cities' with Professor Marcus Thorne and Dr. Sarah Jenkins. Marcus, the term is everywhere, but what does it actually imply for the average citizen?

Marcus: It’s often sold as a high-tech utopia, but at its heart, it’s about efficiency. [15]What I find most compelling isn't the flashy gadgets, but the way data can be used to eliminate the daily frictions of urban life[/15], like traffic congestion or waste management. It’s about making the city 'invisible' in its functioning so that people can actually enjoy living in it.

Interviewer: Sarah, you’ve raised concerns about the 'digital divide'. Are we leaving people behind?

Sarah: Absolutely. If a city requires a smartphone and a high-speed connection just to access basic public services, we are effectively disenfranchising a significant portion of the population. [16]My worry is that the focus on 'smart' technology often ignores the fact that a city must remain accessible to the elderly and the less affluent[/16] who might not be tech-savvy. We risk creating a two-tier society within the same zip code.

Interviewer: Marcus, you’ve worked on the new transit system in Singapore. Was that a success?

Marcus: It was a technical marvel, certainly. But [17]I was actually quite surprised by how much public resistance there was to the automated nature of the buses.[/17] People felt uneasy without a human presence, even when the data showed the AI was safer. It taught us that psychological comfort is just as important as mechanical reliability when implementing change.

Interviewer: Sarah, what about the environmental aspect? Can sensors really save the planet?

Sarah: They can certainly help. For instance, smart lighting that only activates when someone is present saves a fortune in energy. However, [18]I believe the real game-changer is 'predictive maintenance' for water systems[/18]. In many older cities, up to thirty percent of treated water is lost to leaks before it even reaches a tap. Fixing those before they burst is a massive win for sustainability.

Interviewer: Some people find the constant data collection a bit 'Big Brother'. Is privacy a thing of the past?

Marcus: It’s a valid concern. We’re tracking everything from footfall to air quality. But [19]the real danger arises when this data is sold to third parties for targeted advertising rather than being used for public benefit.[/19] We need ironclad laws that ensure the city's data belongs to its citizens, not to the corporations that install the sensors.

Interviewer: Finally, will we all be living in smart cities by 2050?

Sarah: I think the distinction will vanish. [20]Eventually, we won't even use the term 'smart' because this level of integration will be the baseline expectation for any functional habitat.[/20] It will be like running water—you only notice it when it's not there. Marcus and I are just laying the foundations for a world that will eventually feel completely natural to the next generation.

questions.question: What does Marcus find most interesting about the concept of smart cities?
options: A) the impressive nature of the new gadgets
B) the reduction of minor problems in everyday life
C) the aesthetic design of modern urban areas
D) the increased visibility of city management
questions.explanation: Marcus states that what he finds compelling is using data to 'eliminate the daily frictions of urban life'.

questions.question: Sarah expresses concern that smart city technology might
options: A) lead to a decrease in public service funding.
B) be too expensive for local governments to maintain.
C) exclude certain groups from essential services.
D) cause confusion among high-speed internet users.
questions.explanation: She worries about disenfranchising the elderly and less affluent who may not have technology access.

questions.question: Regarding the transit project in Singapore, Marcus was struck by
options: A) the failure of the AI to ensure safety.
B) the speed at which the project was completed.
C) the negative reaction of the public to automation.
D) the lack of data available for the study.
questions.explanation: He says he was 'surprised by how much public resistance there was to the automated nature of the buses'.

questions.question: Sarah believes that the most significant environmental benefit comes from
options: A) the installation of smart street lighting.
B) identifying problems in water networks early.
C) reducing the energy consumption of buildings.
D) treating water more effectively in old cities.
questions.explanation: She calls 'predictive maintenance' for water systems the 'real game-changer' for sustainability.

questions.question: In Marcus's view, the main threat to privacy in smart cities is
options: A) the tracking of air quality in public spaces.
B) the sheer amount of data being collected.
C) the commercial exploitation of personal data.
D) the laws that protect corporate interests.
questions.explanation: He argues the real danger is when data is 'sold to third parties for targeted advertising'.

questions.question: What is Sarah's prediction for the year 2050?
options: A) Smart cities will be replaced by more natural habitats.
B) The term 'smart city' will no longer be necessary.
C) People will finally notice how running water works.
D) The next generation will reject integrated technology.
questions.explanation: She predicts that integration will be such a 'baseline expectation' that we won't even use the term anymore.
